course_description:
The course extends financial decision-making to the international setting. Topics include the foreign exchange market, predicting exchange rate movements, hedging exposures with various derivative and other alternatives, the challenges of raising funds internationally and the process and documents of international trade. The course makes extensive use of case studies and there is an expectation that students will make recommendations to real life problems and justify their decisions. This is a challenging course with a significant theory content which requires seminar attendance and participation in addition to the appropriate independent study and thought to be successful.

This subject introduces and extends financial decision making in an international context. We will introduce exchange rates, observe the impacts and investigate the factors that might explain exchange rate movements. Additionally, we consider strategies to minimise the effects of exchange rate changes. We cover a range of issues pertaining to the globalisation of capital markets, including international investment, multinational cost of capital and capital structure and multinational capital budgeting.

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

No change has been made to the course

Sustainable Development Goals - UQ Business School is a proud supporter and Advanced Signatory of the United Nations Principles for Responsible Management Education (UN PRME) . As part of the largest global collaboration between business schools and the UN, the school emphasises its role in empowering students to drive societal transformation through the Sustainable Development Goals . The SDGs highlight that a thriving economy relies on a healthy environment, aiming to balance economic growth, social well-being, and environmental protection for a sustainable future.

course_aims: This course aims to build on your knowledge of finance by expanding the coverage to firms that operate in the international market.ﾠ

learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate a knowledge of the international monetary system and an ability to calculate and interpret values to various exchange rate problems.
Outline and apply theories of international finance which are used by firms to forecast exchange rates.
Explain foreign exchange exposure and compare instruments and techniques available to manage it.
Apply theories of international finance to case studies so as to analyse and evaluate and then articulate solutions to real world problems.
Compare costs and sources of capital of international and domestic firms.

assessment_details.title: Individual Project
assessment_details.task_description: This is an individual assignment. The project requires students to make a variety of international financial decisions. These may include calculations involving foreign exchange rates and discussions on contemporary issues related to the international monetary system, such as cryptocurrencies, exchange rate regimes, inflation, and other relevant topics. Submission Guidelines: Please ensure that your full name and student number appear on every page of your assignment by inserting a header. Your document must be submitted in PDF format , typed in Times New Roman , font size 12 , and double-spaced with 2.54 cm margins on all sides. Each question should begin on a new page. References must be placed at the end of each question , not at the end of the document. Ensure proper and consistent referencing for each response. Use APA referencing style . As a guide for word count, use a maximum of 150 words per mark . For example, for a 2-mark question, the maximum is 300 words. AI Statement: Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 14 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

learning_activities.topic: Topic 1: Course Intro and The Intl Monetary System Globalisation; four major types of exchange rate mechanisms; digital currencies Learning outcomes: L01
Course Introduction and The Intl Monetary Learning outcomes: L01
Topic 2: The Foreign Exchange Market Characteristics of foreign exchange (forex)markets; terminology and calculations for spot and forward forex markets; triangular arbitrage Learning outcomes: L01
The Foreign Exchange (Forex) Market Learning outcomes: L01

Topic 3: International Parity Conditions Five parity conditions: Purchasing Power Domestic Fisher Effect International Fisher Effect Forward Rate Unbiasedness Interest Rate Parity Learning outcomes: L01, L02
International Parity Conditions Learning outcomes: L01, L02

Topic 4: Balance of Payments (BOP) Determinants of Exchange Rates II: Five accounts: Current Capital Financial Net errors and omissions Official reserves. National Income/Macroeconomic Accounting Identities Learning outcomes: L01, L02
Balance of Payments Learning outcomes: L01, L02
Topic 5: Foreign Currency Derivatives Forex futures and forwards Forex options Single currency / cross currency interest rate swaps Learning outcomes: L01, L03
No Lecture There will be no lecture in Teaching Week 6 to allow students time to prepare for the in-person in-semester exam. Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L04

Mid Sem Exam Revision - Tutorial Tutors will go through practice questions to help students prepare for the in-semester exam. All weekly consultations will remain unchanged unless otherwise specified. Due to Good Friday public holiday, students are advised to attend alternative tutorials. Good Friday Public Holiday - Friday 3rd April 2026 - Check Blackboard for announcements about affected classes.
In-Semester Break
Foreign Currency Derivatives Learning outcomes: L01, L03

Topic 6: Foreign Exchange Exposure 1 Transaction and translation exposures; how are they measured?; methods of hedging transaction exposure (financial hedges vs. operational hedges); translation (accounting) exposure Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L04
Foreign Exchange Exposure 1 Learning outcomes: L01, L03

Topic 7: Foreign Exchange Exposure II Economic (or Operating) exposure: Why and how does it arise? How to measure it? Methods of hedging Economic Exposure Real world examples. Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L04
Foreign Exchange Exposure II Learning outcomes: L03, L04

Topic 8: Intl Portfolio Theory and Diversification Basics and benefits of international diversification (real and simulated datasets) Measuring foreign investment performance Home bias puzzle Learning outcomes: L05
Intl Portfolio Theory and Diversification Learning outcomes: L05

Topic 9: Sourcing Global Capital & Political Risk Capital Market Segmentation Political risk Basics of cost of capital in the international context International bond and equity markets Learning outcomes: L05
Sourcing Global Capital & Political Risk Learning outcomes: L05
Topic 10: International Capital Budgeting Evaluating foreign vs. domestic projects How do they differ? Valuation using parent vs. project cash flows Learning outcomes: L05
International Capital Budgeting Learning outcomes: L05
Guest Lecture/ Special Topics Learning outcomes: L03, L04

Revision Tutorials This week’s tutorials will focus on final exam practice questions. Weekly consultation times will remain unchanged unless otherwise advised. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
Brief course and exam review Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
